category_title: Adolescents with ASD exhibiting attention-seeking out-of-seat and assaultive behaviors
student_description: Brian is a 12-year-old with ASD whose morning lesson difficulties include leaving his table without permission and, at times, physical aggression toward others. His behavior is attention-seeking and often occurs when academic tasks become difficult, especially when he walks toward the door and checks whether adults are watching. The evidence shows that he needs attention to be redirected away from escape/attention bids in the moment, while long-term success depends on highly personalized, collaborative, self-managed contracts rather than standard school rewards.

criteria.rationale: Brian’s long-term improvement began when he worked with his teacher at the start of each day to agree on clear academic targets.
criteria.check: The plan includes regular collaborative target-setting with Brian before lessons, rather than goals being imposed without his involvement.

criteria.rationale: Brian benefits when specific academic completion requirements are clearly connected to access to a preferred activity.
criteria.check: The plan defines observable work or behavior targets and links meeting them to a specific, meaningful reward or activity.

criteria.rationale: Reviewing expectations and rewards and having Brian sign with the teacher helped make the behavior plan clear and jointly owned.
criteria.check: The plan includes a recurring review of expectations and rewards with Brian plus an explicit mutual commitment, such as signing, confirming, or otherwise agreeing to the contract.

criteria.rationale: Brian’s self-management improved when he had freedom to select reinforcers and set appropriate goals, which standard rewards did not reliably provide.
criteria.check: The plan gives Brian meaningful choice over reinforcers and at least some role in shaping his goals.

criteria.rationale: The evidence shows progress when Brian tracked his own progress and began independently returning to his seat during difficult tasks.
criteria.check: The plan requires Brian to monitor or record his own progress in a way that supports independent regulation during challenging work.

criteria.rationale: Brian’s behavior returned to baseline when the contract relied only on school-setting reinforcement whose novelty and value faded.
criteria.check: The plan includes a way to keep reinforcers personally valuable over time and does not rely solely on generic or fixed school-based rewards.
